what_it_does: A skeptical trust assessment of whether a strong model can reliably compile freeform natural-language intent into a valid, runnable, law-abiding chain configuration. It emits real Chain instances for four intent classes, inspects them adversarially, dissects each proposed guardrail for what it buys and does not buy, tiers intents by auto-compile safety, and answers explicit deliverable questions about compiler trust.
observations: Uses an evidence-tagging discipline: Verified, Observed, Inferred, External-prior-art, and [my idea].
Runs four empirical probes (A-D) by emitting concrete Chain objects over the real company repo and then reviewing them with an approver hat.

Probe A is a clean per-unit classification chain; Probe B shows an ambiguous scope guess plus a non-unit-local field; Probe C plants a subtle non-unit-local clause that reads like a per-unit clause; Probe D shows a cross-unit intent for which single-map is the wrong primitive.
Argues a structural asymmetry with SEM-3: the semantic layer has a stronger-model confirm keystone, but COMPILE has no model tier above the compiler, so that keystone is unavailable.
Dissects five trust legs: schema-constrain, inspect-approve, validate-before-run, dry-run-one-unit, and cheap pre-map grounding, each with explicit buy/does-not-buy claims.
Names the dominant failure mode as a schema-valid, reads-fine Chain that encodes the wrong question, especially a non-unit-local map_prompt.
Proposes a three-tier verdict: T1 auto-compile-and-run-safe, T2 compile-but-must-be-approved, T3 should not be auto-compiled at all.
Recommends building a compile calibration harness from the anchor's five faces plus the four probes before trusting auto-compile.
Highlights the save-config-not-intent insight: the non-deterministic compile is paid once for saved faces, while the runner over a fixed Chain is reproducible.

Identifies reuse opportunities in existing runtime machinery: governance.py posture system, authoring.py dry_run_role pattern, implement.py structured-result discipline, and compile.py deterministic validation model.
Includes a 'yes, but actually' section that sharpens or corrects claims in the companion anchor document.
Cites external prior art including text-to-SQL semantic-error rates and structured-output false-confidence literature.
standouts: The missing-keystone structural asymmetry: COMPILE has no stronger model above it, so SEM-3's confirm leg is structurally unavailable.
Probe C's planted non-unit-local map_prompt, which is textually invisible in the plan and therefore not catchable by inspect-approve alone.
Dry-run-one-unit proposed as the only cheap empirical leg that surfaces non-unit-local prompts.
The explicit T3 class of intents that should never be auto-compiled because the map primitive itself is wrong.
The reuse discovery that inspect-approve, dry-run, and structured-result disciplines already exist in runtime/governance.py, runtime/authoring.py, and runtime/implement.py.
conventions: Follows the evidence-marking template from Tim's template.
Heavy use of file:line references and internal document section citations.
Self-aware epistemic limits, e.g. n=4, self as both compiler and approver.
Tiered verdict structure (T1/T2/T3) matching SEM-3's S1/S2/S3 gradient.
Inline code blocks for emitted Chain instances.
Explicit 'buy / does not buy' framing for each guardrail.
Neutral, interpretive tone with no ranking of files or ideas.
concerns: The empirical probe is n=4 and self-conducted by the same model as both compiler and approver, which the file itself flags as a blind-spot caveat.
Many of the proposed guardrails are tagged [my idea] and are not yet built or verified in this file.
The line between Observed and Inferred is sometimes subtle and may be hard for a reader to track.
The file is very long and dense, which could reduce actionability despite its explicit build list.
It references many external papers and internal files without reproducing their content, so verification depends on reading outside this file.
No actual code, schema, or harness artifact is included; it is entirely a prose/design assessment.
notes: A skeptical, tiered trust assessment of an NL-to-chain compiler that argues the compile stage lacks a stronger-model keystone and must rely on validate-before-run, dry-run-one-unit, human inspect-approve, and a hard do-not-auto-compile boundary for cross-unit reasoning.
questions: What is the exact Chain schema referenced as anchor §3, and where is it defined?
What are the specific intent→Chain pairs for the five faces mentioned in anchor §3?
Has any part of Chain.validate(), dry-run-one-unit, or the calibration harness been implemented yet?
What is the precise machine-checkable definition of 'law-abiding' beyond the runnable layer?
How will T1/T2/T3 classification be decided automatically for a novel intent?
What is the latency and cost trade-off of running a dry-run on one unit for every compile?
What does the governance.py posture API look like for adding a new compile/run-chain action class?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches — the stated purpose is to assess compiler trustworthiness skeptically, and the file does so through empirical probes, guardrail dissection, tiering, and concrete build recommendations.
apparent_themes: Structural trust asymmetry and the missing keystone
Unit-locality as a law of the map primitive
Save the config, not the intent
Reuse existing runtime guardrails rather than rebuild them
intention_signals: It is trying to prevent an over-claim that an NL-to-chain compiler is broadly safe, to establish honest automation boundaries, to map SEM-3's trust ladder onto the compile stage while exposing where that ladder breaks, and to turn the assessment into a concrete build and calibration plan.
novelty: The explicit framing that the compile stage has no stronger-model keystone, the planted non-unit-local prompt probe, the dry-run-one-unit as a keystone substitute, the T3 do-not-auto-compile class, and the reuse discovery that the needed guardrail patterns already exist in governance/authoring/implement/compile runtime modules.
contribution: A skeptical, tiered framework for deciding when an NL-to-chain compiler can be trusted; a probe methodology for exposing schema-valid-but-wrong-question plans; a dissection of what each guardrail actually buys and does not buy; a T1/T2/T3 classification for intents; a specification for a compile calibration harness; and a mapping of the needed guardrails to existing runtime machinery.
summary_for_embedding: CC-2 is a skeptical trust assessment of a natural-language-to-chain compiler, using four emitted-Chain probes to argue that the compile stage lacks a stronger-model keystone and therefore must rely on validate-before-run, dry-run-one-unit, inspect-approve, and a do-not-auto-compile tier for cross-unit reasoning.
intention_for_embedding: It was written to set honest, measured boundaries for auto-compiling freeform intent into runnable chain configs and to guide which guardrails to build first.
